Curriculum → Common product design patterns → Search and autocomplete

Lesson 3 of 5

Search and autocomplete

Search systems combine ingestion, indexing, ranking, caching, and query-time latency controls.

25-35 min Common product design patterns 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Autocomplete is a strong interview prompt because it requires you to reason about prefix retrieval, freshness, and hot terms.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Separate source of truth from search index Use CDC or async indexing pipelines2. Autocomplete data structures Cache popular prefixes aggressively3. Latency and relevance Precompute signals when possible

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Autocomplete is a strong interview prompt because it requires you to reason about prefix retrieval, freshness, and hot terms. 2. Separate source of truth from search index Use CDC or async indexing pipelines3. Autocomplete data structures Cache popular prefixes aggressively4. Latency and relevance Precompute signals when possible
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Autocomplete is a strong interview prompt because it requires you to reason about prefix retrieval, freshness, and hot terms.

What to say first

  • Keep search indexes derived from the source of truth.
  • Choose an autocomplete structure based on update and query shape.
  • Cache hot prefixes and trending results.
2

Study section

Separate source of truth from search index

Search indexes are derived data optimized for retrieval, not the canonical record.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use CDC or async indexing pipelines
  • Expect eventual consistency between the primary store and the index
  • Track indexing lag and failed documents
3

Study section

Autocomplete data structures

Tries, prefix indexes, n-grams, or specialized search engines can power autocomplete depending on scale and update frequency.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cache popular prefixes aggressively
  • Use ranking signals such as popularity and personalization
  • Protect the service from high-QPS short queries
4

Study section

Latency and relevance

Search must trade response time against ranking sophistication. Good answers call out where expensive ranking moves offline.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Precompute signals when possible
  • Use top-k retrieval before deep ranking
  • Fallback to trending suggestions when dependencies lag

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Keep search indexes derived from the source of truth.
  • Choose an autocomplete structure based on update and query shape.
  • Cache hot prefixes and trending results.
  • Observe freshness, latency, and zero-result rates.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Writing directly to the search index and calling it the source of truth.
  • Ignoring abusive high-QPS prefix queries.
  • Trying to do all ranking work synchronously in the request path.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you keep autocomplete fresh after updates?
  • What should happen when the index is behind?
  • Why is caching so effective for short-prefix search?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

The NoSQL landscape Data storage and indexingObservability and operational feedback loops Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Search and autocomplete

Use product-shaped traffic to explain where feeds, search, uploads, or messaging systems really bend. Apply it to search and autocomplete so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0.0 KB markdown

Explain the concept

Teach "Search and autocomplete" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would you keep autocomplete fresh after updates?
  • Separate source of truth from search index
  • Autocomplete data structures
  • Keep search indexes derived from the source of truth.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs
